Title: BenLLM-Eval: A Comprehensive Evaluation into the Potentials and Pitfalls of Large Language Models on Bengali NLP
Authors: Mohsinul Kabir, Mohammed Saidul Islam, Md Tahmid Rahman Laskar, Mir Tafseer Nayeem, M Saiful Bari, Enamul Hoque
Official Venue: Proceedings of the 2024 Joint International Conference on Computational Linguistics, Language Resources and Evaluation (LREC-COLING 2024)
Problem: LLMs not benchmarked on Bengali NLP tasks
Task Type: Multi-task evaluation (summarization / QA / paraphrasing / NLI / text classification / sentiment)
Dataset: Custom evaluation set across 6 Bengali NLP tasks (specific dataset names not clearly stated)
Method: Zero-shot evaluation: ChatGPT (GPT-3.5), LLaMA-2-13b-chat, Claude-2
Main Result: Zero-shot LLMs match/exceed SOTA on a few tasks (e.g. sentiment) but underperform fine-tuned models on most; LLaMA-2 worst
Limitation: Zero-shot only; 3 LLMs; no fine-tuned/instruction-tuned comparison; undisclosed LLM training data limits contamination check
Future Work: Expand experiments to more low/modest-resource languages, tasks, datasets, and settings

Title: An Empirical Study on the Characteristics of Bias upon Context Length Variation for Bangla
Authors: Jayanta Sadhu, Ayan Khan, Abhik Bhattacharjee, Rifat Shahriyar
Official Venue: Findings of the Association for Computational Linguistics: ACL 2024
Problem: Social/gender bias in Bangla pretrained LMs unstudied; context-length effect on bias measurement unexplored
Dataset: New Bangla intrinsic gender-bias dataset (size not clearly stated)
Method: Adapted WEAT/SEAT intrinsic bias metrics for Bangla; context-length variation experiments
Main Result: Bias metrics show clear dependency on context length — a factor overlooked in prior work
Limitation: Gender bias only; intrinsic measures only; adapted/synthetic datasets; no extrinsic downstream evaluation
Future Work: Study bias in downstream applications; develop language-specific debiasing; extend to generative models and other bias types (social, religious, political)

Title: BanglaTLit: A Benchmark Dataset for Back-Transliteration of Romanized Bangla
Authors: Md Fahim, Fariha Tanjim Shifat, Fabiha Haider, Deeparghya Dutta Barua, MD Sakib Ul Rahman Sourove, Md Farhan Ishmam, Md Farhad Alam Bhuiyan
Official Venue: Findings of the Association for Computational Linguistics: EMNLP 2024
Problem: Romanized Bangla abundant online but untapped; no large-scale back-transliteration benchmark
Dataset: BanglaTLit: 42.7k samples (38.7k/1.5k/2.5k train/val/test); BanglaTLit-PT pre-training corpus: 245.7k samples
Method: TB-Encoders (encoders further pre-trained on BanglaTLit-PT) + dual encoder-decoder (TB-encoder-aggregated T5)
Evaluation Metric: BLEU, ROUGE, METEOR, BERTScore (back-transliteration); Accuracy/F1 (downstream classification)
Main Result: Best model TB-XLM_R + BanglaT5_NMT: BLEU 36.07, ROUGE-1 78.92, METEOR 78.14, BERTScore 98.82; TB-encoders reach SOTA on romanized Bangla classification
Limitation: Source data (TrickBd) is single-domain (tech-support); informal romanized text noisy
Future Work: Expand dataset for larger models / LLM fine-tuning; dialectal transliteration; parameter-efficient fine-tuning of LLMs

Title: BhasaBodh: Bridging Bangla Dialects and Romanized Forms through Machine Translation
Authors: Md. Tofael Ahmed Bhuiyan, Md. Abdur Rahman, Abdul Kadar Muhammad Masum
Official Venue: Proceedings of the Second Workshop on Bangla Language Processing (BLP-2025)
Problem: Bangla dialects (Chittagong, Sylhet) and romanized forms underserved in MT; no sentence-level benchmark
Dataset: Multi-way sentence-level parallel corpus: 980 sentences per dialect (Chittagong, Sylhet) aligned with Standard Bangla and English, plus romanized versions
Method: Fine-tuned NLLB-200 and mBART-50 on 7 translation tasks
Main Result: mBART-50 outperforms NLLB-200 on most tasks; best BLEU 87.44 on Romanized-to-Standard Bangla normalization (authors note synthetic uniformity inflates this score)
Limitation: Modest scale (980 sentences/dialect); synthetic romanized data; small non-expert manual validation without inter-annotator agreement; only 2 models; automatic metrics only; only 2 dialects
Future Work: Human evaluation; benchmark 7B-13B models (few-shot + fine-tuning); expand with authentic romanized text; multi-dialect transfer learning; dialect-aware pretraining

Title: BLUCK: A Benchmark Dataset for Bengali Linguistic Understanding and Cultural Knowledge
Authors: Daeen Kabir, Minhajur Rahman Chowdhury Mahim, Sheikh Shafayat, Adnan Sadik, Arian Ahmed, Eunsu Kim, Alice Oh
Official Venue: Proceedings of the Second Workshop on Bangla Language Processing (BLP-2025)
Problem: No benchmark to test LLM knowledge of Bengali culture / history / linguistics
Task Type: MCQ Benchmark / Cultural QA / LLM Evaluation
Dataset: BLUCK: 2,366 MCQs across 23 subcategories / 4 domains (Bangladesh culture, history, Bengali linguistics)
Method: Evaluation of 9 LLMs (6 proprietary + 3 open-source): GPT-4o, Claude-3.5-Sonnet, Gemini-1.5-Pro, Llama-3.3-70B-Instruct, DeepSeekV3, others
Main Result: Models reasonably good overall but weak in Bengali phonetics / linguistic nuances; all below English; Bengali positioned as mid-resource
Limitation: Text-only MCQ (cannot inspect reasoning); 23 categories may not cover all domains; small vs M-MMLU/Global-MMLU; no open-ended evaluation
Future Work: Expand BLUCK; improve LLMs' understanding of Bengali linguistic and cultural nuances

Title: BanHate: An Up-to-Date and Fine-Grained Bangla Hate Speech Dataset
Authors: Faisal Hossain Raquib, Akm Moshiur Rahman Mazumder, Md Tahmid Hasan Fuad, Md Farhan Ishmam, Md Fahim
Official Venue: Proceedings of the Second Workshop on Bangla Language Processing (BLP-2025)
Problem: Existing Bangla hate speech resources outdated, binary-labeled, miss evolving implicit hate
Task Type: Hate Speech Detection (binary + 7 categories + 7 target groups)
Dataset: BanHate: 19,203 YouTube comments (Apr 2024 - Jun 2025); binary + 7 categories + 7 target groups
Method: Prompting (zero-shot, CoT) + LoRA fine-tuning on 5 open-source (Qwen-2.5-7B, Gemma-3-12B, Llama-3.1-8B, Phi-4-14B, Mistral-7B) and 2 closed (GPT-4o, Gemini-2.5-Flash) LLMs
Evaluation Metric: Precision, Recall, F1 (Micro/Macro), Subset Accuracy, Hamming loss
Main Result: LoRA fine-tuning gives most consistent gains; LLaMA-3.1-8B (LoRA) best (Non-Hate F1 85.96 / Hate F1 83.83); GPT-4o/Gemini strong on binary but weak on implicit/fine-grained hate
Limitation: YouTube top-level comments only (single platform); single LoRA epoch, greedy decoding, no held-out dev set or multi-seed runs
Future Work: Not clearly stated

Title: BanHateME: Understanding Hate in Bangla Memes thorough Detection, Categorization, and Target Profiling
Authors: Md Ayon Mia, Md Fahim
Official Venue: Proceedings of the Second Workshop on Bangla Language Processing (BLP-2025)
Problem: No Bangla multimodal hate meme dataset; prior resources only binary-labeled
Dataset: BanHateME: 3,819 Bangla memes (Facebook 2,517 + Instagram 1,302; train/val/test 2,673/381/765); 3-level hierarchical labels (binary, 5 hate categories, 4 target groups)
Method: Pretrained language + vision encoders (BanglaBERT/XLM-RoBERTa x ViT/Swin); fusion: summation/concatenation/co-attention; hierarchical loss
Main Result: BanglaBERT+Swin with concatenation best on binary (F1 74.08 / Acc 74.51); co-attention best for target-group recognition; hierarchical loss (alpha=beta=0.5) most balanced
Limitation: Modest dataset size; pretrained encoders not Bangla-optimized; subtle cultural cues hard for models
Future Work: Expand dataset with efficient annotation; explore culturally-adapted multimodal architectures; experiment with LVLMs and prompting

Title: CheckSent-BN: A Bengali Multi-Task Dataset for Claim Checkworthiness and Sentiment Classification from News Headlines
Authors: Pritam Pal, Dipankar Das
Official Venue: Proceedings of the Second Workshop on Bangla Language Processing (BLP-2025)
Problem: No Bengali dataset for claim checkworthiness + sentiment; no joint benchmark
Dataset: CheckSent-BN: 11,568 Bengali news headlines (West Bengal, India sources); checkworthy 9,254 / non-checkworthy 2,314; LLM-annotated (GPT-4o-mini, GPT-4.1-mini + 1) with manual verification
Method: 8 multilingual transformer models in single-task (STL) and multi-task (MTL); IndicBERTv2, BanglaBERT, mDeBERTa strongest
Main Result: IndicBERTv2-based MTL framework best overall; STL better for positive sentiment
Limitation: LLM-annotated labels may be noisy; 'mini' GPT variants; class imbalance; West-Bengal headlines only (excludes Bangladesh/Tripura/Assam); single domain (headlines)
Future Work: Expand sample size; compare LLM vs human annotation; add clickbait/sarcasm labels; hire professional annotators; balance dataset; add Tripura/Assam/Bangladesh headlines

Title: BanglaTalk: Towards Real-Time Speech Assistance for Bengali Regional Dialects
Authors: Jakir Hasan, Shubhashis Roy Dipta
Official Venue: Proceedings of the Second Workshop on Bangla Language Processing (BLP-2025)
Problem: No real-time speech assistant for Bengali regional dialects
Dataset: RegSpeech12 (spans 12 Bangladeshi regions; BRDialect trained on 10 dialects); TTS evaluated on a BanSpeech subset
Method: BRDialect: IndicWav2Vec fine-tuned on 10 dialects; client-server RTP architecture; VITS-Bengali TTS
Main Result: BRDialect beats baseline ASR by 12.41-33.98% on RegSpeech12 (absolute WER 74.1% / CER 40.6%); TTS MOS 4.49; 4.9s end-to-end delay at 24 kbps
Limitation: 4.9s latency limits conversational use; only 10 of 12 regions trained; no user-interruption handling
Future Work: Add user-interruption handling; incorporate speech data from remaining regions of Bangladesh

Title: Reveal-Bangla: A Dataset for Cross-Lingual Multi-Step Reasoning Evaluation
Authors: Khondoker Ittehadul Islam, Gabriele Sarti
Official Venue: Proceedings of the Second Workshop on Bangla Language Processing (BLP-2025)
Problem: Multi-step reasoning in Bangla unevaluated; unclear if models exploit Bangla reasoning chains
Dataset: Reveal-Bangla: manually translated subset of English Reveal — 104 questions, 188 evidence paragraphs, 355 reasoning steps (751 translated texts; ~70% binary)
Method: Controlled evaluation of Llama-3.2-1B-Instruct (EngLlama) and BanglaLLama-3.2-1b (BenLlama); answers verified by mDeBERTa-v3 NLI model
Main Result: Reasoning context benefits non-binary questions; models struggle to use Bangla reasoning steps (CoT gains larger in English than Bangla)
Limitation: Small (104 questions); ~70% binary skew; only ~1B SLMs; single-annotator translation; NLI-verifier limitations; no GPT-4-class model
Future Work: Test whether trends hold with larger models; explore model interpretability in low-resource languages

Title: Too Late to Train, Too Early To Use? A Study on Necessity and Viability of Low-Resource Bengali LLMs
Authors: Tamzeed Mahfuz, Satak Kumar Dey, Ruwad Naswan, Hasnaen Adil, Khondker Salman Sayeed, Haz Sameen Shahgir
Official Venue: Proceedings of the 31st International Conference on Computational Linguistics (COLING 2025)
Problem: Whether a dedicated Bengali-oriented LLM is still necessary given improving cross-lingual transfer in English-centric LLMs
Task Type: Multi-task LLM evaluation (translation, summarization, paraphrase, QA, NLI, reward modeling)
Dataset: Existing Bengali test sets: BanglaNMT, XLSum, CrossSum, BanglaParaphrase, SQuAD-bn, BanglaRQA, BEnQA, XNLI-bn
Method: Zero-shot LLaMA-3-8B/70B, Mistral-7B, Aya-23, Qwen-2-72B, GPT-3.5/4/4o, Gemini-1.5-Pro, NLLB vs fine-tuned BanglaT5-248M and BanglaBERT-111M; plus QLoRA fine-tuned LLaMA-3-8B
Evaluation Metric: BLEU, ROUGE-2, F1, Exact Match, Accuracy
Main Result: LLMs lead on QA/reasoning (LLaMA-3-70B SQuAD-bn F1 81.9; GPT-4 BEnQA 75.1) but fine-tuned BanglaT5 still wins paraphrase (BLEU 32.80) and monolingual summarization (ROUGE-2 13.7); Bengali tokenization highly inefficient (~0.85 char/token vs ~4.5 English)
Limitation: No human evaluation; fine-tuning limited mainly to LLaMA-3-8B; findings may become outdated
Future Work: Develop efficient tokenization for non-Latin scripts; create high-quality Bengali datasets; exploit cross-lingual transfer

Title: Can LLMs be Literary Companions?: Analysing LLMs on Bengali Figures of Speech Identification
Authors: Sourav Das, Kripabandhu Ghosh
Official Venue: Proceedings of the 2025 Conference on Empirical Methods in Natural Language Processing (EMNLP 2025)
Problem: Identifying figures of speech in Bengali literary text is underexplored; computational FoS approaches for Bengali virtually nonexistent
Dataset: BengFoS: 3,148 expert-annotated literary sentences (from 10,198 collected) across 6 Bengali poets; 13 FoS labels; Cohen's kappa 0.78
Method: Zero-shot then fine-tuned Llama-3-8B and DeepSeek-R1-Distill-7B (full/LoRA, 8/16-bit); SVM and Naive Bayes baselines; layer-wise linear probing
Evaluation Metric: Accuracy, Precision, Recall, micro/macro/weighted F1 (5-fold CV)
Main Result: Zero-shot weak (Llama-3 F1 0.42); best deployed fine-tuned DeepSeek-R1-7B (16-bit) weighted-F1 0.64 (Llama-3 0.40); probing peaks Llama-3 micro-F1 0.624, DeepSeek 0.575; gains remain modest
Limitation: Only 6 poets (authorial skew); class imbalance with near-zero recall on rare FoS; prose lacks FoS; length sensitivity
Future Work: Incorporate synthetic discourse contexts or span-level augmentation to improve underrepresented labels

Title: BanglaByT5: Byte-Level Modelling for Bangla
Authors: Pramit Bhattacharyya, Arnab Bhattacharya
Official Venue: Findings of the Association for Computational Linguistics: EMNLP 2025
Problem: Subword tokenizers (BPE/SentencePiece) fragment morphologically rich Bangla; no byte-level Bangla model existed
Task Type: Byte-level language modelling; generative + classification tasks
Dataset: 14 GB pretraining corpus (VACASPATI literature + IndicCorp-Bangla news); downstream: sentiment, NER (Naamapadam-bn), MT, paraphrase, GEC (VAIYAKARANA)
Method: BanglaByT5 (~300M params, ByT5-small variant, byte-level span-corruption pretraining) vs mT5, ByT5, GPT-2, BLOOM, IndicBART, BanglaT5, Paramanu
Evaluation Metric: macro-F1, sacreBLEU, GLEU, BERTScore, BLEU, METEOR, LLM-as-judge
Main Result: Outperforms similar-size IndicBART/BanglaT5 and larger BLOOM-1.1B; within ~5% of GPT2-XL despite being ~5x smaller; sentiment 68.30, NER 33.60, MT (sacreBLEU) 24.36, paraphrase 35.78, GEC (GLEU) 66.27
Limitation: Limited (14 GB) data forced a small variant; possible hallucination; memorization inherent (here minimal, EM 0.00)
Future Work: Not clearly stated

Title: BanNERD: A Benchmark Dataset and Context-Driven Approach for Bangla Named Entity Recognition
Authors: Md. Motahar Mahtab, Faisal Ahamed Khan, Md. Ekramul Islam, Md. Shahad Mahmud Chowdhury, Labib Imam Chowdhury, Sadia Afrin, Hazrat Ali, Mohammad Mamun Or Rashid, Nabeel Mohammed, Mohammad Ruhul Amin
Official Venue: Findings of the Association for Computational Linguistics: NAACL 2025
Problem: Bangla NER lacks large quality datasets; existing resources noisy; context-concatenation approaches truncate external context
Dataset: BanNERD: 85,175 sentences, 29 domains, 10 NER classes, IAA 0.88; 51 annotators + 5 validators
Method: BanNERCEM (context-ensemble: each retrieved Wikipedia context passed separately + hierarchical entity-aware majority voting); PLM (BanglaBERT/XLM-R) + CRF
Main Result: 90.49% macro-F1 on BanNERD; new SOTA on 5 of 6 prior Bangla NER datasets (~5.67% higher avg macro-F1 than second-best cross-dataset); English MultiCoNER I 90.34% (abstract also reports 81.85% avg macro-F1 across 10 classes; Sec 6.1 reports 84.12% highest average)
Limitation: Per-context passing increases training/inference time; 'Creative Work' labels weakest on MultiCoNER
Future Work: Incorporate more knowledge bases; explore more efficient context utilization to reduce training/inference time

Title: Bengali ChartSumm: A Benchmark Dataset and study on feasibility of Large Language Models on Bengali Chart to Text Summarization
Authors: Nahida Akter Tanjila, Afrin Sultana Poushi, Sazid Abdullah Farhan, Abu Raihan Mostofa Kamal, Md. Azam Hossain, Md. Hamjajul Ashmafee
Official Venue: Proceedings of the First Workshop on Challenges in Processing South Asian Languages (CHiPSAL 2025)
Problem: No Bengali chart-to-text summarization benchmark; LLMs not yet applied to Bengali chart summarization
Dataset: Bengali ChartSumm: 4,100 Bengali chart images + metadata + summaries (bar/line charts; machine-translated from English then human-annotated; 70/10.5/19.5 split)
Method: mT5-small, BanglaT5, Gemma-2b fine-tuned on title+metadata to caption
Main Result: mT5 best BLEU 0.2779 and lowest errors (CER 0.5295 / WER 0.7189); BanglaT5 best ROUGE-1 0.0678 (+63% over mT5); all ROUGE-2/L scores low
Limitation: Only bar/line charts; limited compute; small homogeneous annotator pool (five students)
Future Work: Bengali OCR; more chart types; SOTA LLMs (LLaMA/Mistral); cross-lingual comparisons; interactive tools

Title: GRASP-ChoQ: Knowledge Graph-Based Retrieval Augmentation for Stance Detection in Political Texts with Chain-of-Questions Reasoning
Authors: Rasel Mahmud, Md. Abdur Rakib Mollah, Aninda Kumar Sharma, Omar Faruq Osama
Official Venue: Proceedings of the Second Workshop on Bangla Language Processing (BLP-2025)
Problem: Political stance detection in low-resource Bangladesh context unstudied; LLMs struggle with dynamic political contexts and indirect entity relations
Dataset: BPDisC: 2,847 Bangladesh-politics tweets (1,512 favor / 1,335 against), language tagged Bangla-English; plus 6 non-Bangla benchmark datasets
Method: GRASP-ChoQ: Neo4j knowledge-graph retrieval augmentation + Chain-of-Questions reasoning over GPT-4/4o, Gemini-1.5-Pro, Mistral-Large, DeepSeek-R1 (non-English translated to English via GPT-4o)
Main Result: DeepSeek-R1 + GRASP-ChoQ best on BPDisC: F1 93.33 (+40.6 over zero-shot); consistent gains across all 7 datasets
Limitation: Text-only (no multimodal); no fine-tuning; limited knowledge base; political sarcasm hard; small inter-annotator samples
Future Work: Not clearly stated

Title: Read Between the Lines: A Benchmark for Uncovering Political Bias in Bangla News Articles
Authors: Nusrat Jahan Lia, Shubhashis Roy Dipta, Abdullah Khan Zehady, Naymul Islam, Madhusodan Chakraborty, Abdullah Al Wasif
Official Venue: Proceedings of the Second Workshop on Bangla Language Processing (BLP-2025)
Problem: Annotated datasets for Bangla political bias scarce; detecting government-affiliation bias in Bangla news is challenging
Task Type: Political bias / stance detection (ternary: Government Leaning / Critique / Neutral)
Dataset: BanglaBias: 200 annotated Bangla news articles; 46 events, 54 sources; 3 annotators (Cohen's kappa 0.574)
Method: Evaluation of 28 LLMs (Qwen, LLaMA, GLM, DeepSeek, GPT, Claude, Gemini, TigerLLM) in reasoning-then-decision prompting; zero-shot vs few-shot; no training
Main Result: Best weighted-F1 Qwen3-235B 0.74, LLaMA-3.3-70B 0.73; Government-Critique easiest (F1 up to 0.83), Neutral hardest (down to 0.00); models over-predict government-leaning; few-shot lagged zero-shot for large models
Limitation: Small/possibly non-representative corpus; data-contamination risk; text-only; limited few-shot compute
Future Work: More diverse sources; contamination checks; multimodal stance detection; fine-tuning; extend to other low-resource languages

Title: BiCap: Bangla Image Captioning Using Attention-based Encoder-Decoder Architecture
Authors: Md Aminul Kader Bulbul
Official Venue: Proceedings of the Second Workshop on Bangla Language Processing (BLP-2025)
Problem: Bangla image captioning limited; no large multimodal corpora/pretrained vision-language models; prior captions suffer repetition and semantic incompleteness
Dataset: Chitron (existing, Sazzed 2020): 15,438 image-caption pairs (12,350/1,544/1,544)
Method: Attention-based encoder-decoder: ResNet-50 encoder + Bahdanau attention + LSTM decoder
Evaluation Metric: BLEU-1..4, METEOR, ROUGE, CIDEr; human evaluation
Main Result: BLEU-4 32.73 (+10 over strongest baseline), METEOR 0.37, ROUGE 0.42, CIDEr 0.29; best human scores (fluency 4.3 / adequacy 4.4 / relevance 4.6)
Limitation: ResNet-50 captures local not global features; sequential LSTM decoder; generalization to unseen domains unverified
Future Work: CNN-ViT hybrids; transformer decoders; more datasets; subword/morphological tokenization

Title: P6Jiggasha: Benchmarking Large Language Models on Bangla Physics Question Answering with Cross-lingual Evaluation
Authors: S.M. Shahriar, Md Tahmid Hasan Fuad, Md Fahim, Md. Azad Hossain
Official Venue: Proceedings of the Second Workshop on Bangla Language Processing (BLP-2025)
Problem: LLM performance on Bangla scientific (physics) QA and cross-lingual consistency unmeasured
Dataset: P6Jiggasha: 1,500 Bangla physics MCQs (HSC textbooks/guides/question banks; 4 options; 6 topics, 6 question types; 500 test) + English translations
Method: GPT-4.1, Gemini-2.5-Pro, DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Llama-70B; zero-shot vs CoT; Bangla vs English
Main Result: GPT-4.1 best Bangla Acc@1 86.67% (Gemini 71.24, DeepSeek 48.00); Gemini reaches 89.52% at Acc@4; GPT-4.1 near-identical Bangla vs English (86.67 vs 84.57); question-type complexity drives gaps more than language
Limitation: MCQ-only; no diagrams; physics-only; cross-lingual inconsistencies; translation may introduce subtle shifts
Future Work: Open-ended formats; multimodal (diagrams); other STEM domains and low-resource languages

Title: ChakmaBridge: A Five-Way Parallel Corpus for Navigating the Script Divide in an Endangered Language
Authors: Md. Abdur Rahman, Md. Tofael Ahmed Bhuiyan, Abdul Kadar Muhammad Masum
Official Venue: Proceedings of the Second Workshop on Bangla Language Processing (BLP-2025)
Problem: Endangered Chakma language lacks standardized parallel corpora linking romanized and native-script forms; MT for Chakma nascent (Bangla used as major source/pivot)
Task Type: Parallel corpus construction + machine translation (endangered language)
Dataset: ChakmaBridge: 807 sentences in 5 forms (English, Standard Bangla, Bengali-script Chakma, Romanized Bangla, Romanized Chakma); 564/81/162 split
Method: Gemini-2.5-Pro generated romanizations (human-validated); mBART-50 and NLLB-200 fine-tuned across 6 translation tasks
Main Result: Best (English+Standard Bangla)->Chakma with NLLB-200: BLEU 0.5228, METEOR 0.6486, BERTScore-F1 0.9136 (+124% BLEU over next-best task); Bangla as source beats English; NLLB-200 generally beats mBART-50
Limitation: Only 807 sentences; LLM-romanization cleaner than real-world usage; validation on only 20 sentences; no native Chakma (Ajha Path) script
Future Work: Expand data; back-translation; native Chakma script support; constrained decoding
